edges.description: Impaired pyruvate oxidation and respiratory-chain flux shift metabolism toward anaerobic glycolysis, raising lactate.
Energy failure preferentially injures metabolically active neurons of the basal ganglia and brainstem.
Reduced complex I activity lowers respiratory-chain flux and ATP output.
Loss of cytochrome c oxidase blocks terminal electron transport.
Defective ATP synthase coupling decreases ATP generation.
Blocked pyruvate oxidation diverts pyruvate to lactate.
Reduced acetyl-CoA limits TCA-cycle reducing equivalents for the respiratory chain.
Systemic and CNS acidosis compounds energetic stress on vulnerable neurons.
Progressive lesion accumulation drives loss of acquired milestones.

nodes.description: Isolated deficiency of NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase (respiratory chain complex I) is the single most common biochemical cause of Leigh syndrome, arising from mtDNA MT-ND subunit variants or nuclear NDUFS/NDUFV/NDUFA subunit and assembly-factor defects.

biological_processes: mitochondrial electron transport, NADH to ubiquinone

evidence.reference_title: Structural insights into respiratory complex I deficiency and assembly from the mitochondrial disease-related ndufs4(-/-) mouse.
evidence.snippet: Complex I mutations cause neuromuscular, mitochondrial diseases, such as Leigh Syndrome, but their molecular-level consequences remain poorly understood.

evidence.reference_title: Structural insights into respiratory complex I deficiency and assembly from the mitochondrial disease-related ndufs4(-/-) mouse.
evidence.snippet: Cryo-EM analyses of the complex I from ndufs4-/- mouse hearts revealed a loose association of the NADH-dehydrogenase module

nodes.description: Cytochrome c oxidase deficiency, classically from SURF1 assembly-factor loss or LRPPRC (French-Canadian Leigh) defects, is a major cause of Leigh syndrome with reduced terminal electron transfer to oxygen.

biological_processes: mitochondrial respiratory chain complex IV assembly

evidence.reference_title: Mutations of SURF-1 in Leigh disease associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Sequence analysis of SURF-1 revealed mutations in numerous DNA samples from LD(COX-) patients, indicating that this gene is responsible for the major complementation group in this important mitochondrial disorder.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of a gene causing human cytochrome c oxidase deficiency by integrative genomics.
evidence.snippet: Resequencing identified two mutations on two independent haplotypes, providing definitive genetic proof that LRPPRC indeed causes LSFC.

nodes.description: Loss of previously acquired motor and cognitive milestones is a defining feature.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Decompensation (often with elevated lactate levels in blood and/or cerebrospinal fluid) is typically associated with developmental delay and/or regression.

nodes.description: Basal-ganglia injury produces dystonia and other movement abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Neurologic features include hypotonia, spasticity, seizures, movement disorders, cerebellar ataxia, and peripheral neuropathy.

nodes.description: Chronic energy deficiency injures metabolically demanding neurons and glia, producing the characteristic bilateral symmetric necrotic lesions, capillary proliferation, and gliosis of the basal ganglia, thalamus, and brainstem that define Leigh syndrome neuropathology.

evidence.reference_title: NARP-MILS syndrome caused by 8993 T>G mitochondrial DNA mutation: a clinical, genetic and neuropathological study.
evidence.snippet: Post-mortem studies of the brain in one affected member clinically presenting with a neurological disorder intermediate between adult Leigh's syndrome and NARP showed symmetrical lesions of the basal ganglia and brainstem closely resembling those usually described in typical Leigh's syndrome.

meta.association: Biallelic LRPPRC variants cause the French-Canadian (Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean) type of cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV)-deficient Leigh syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of a gene causing human cytochrome c oxidase deficiency by integrative genomics.
evidence.snippet: Resequencing identified two mutations on two independent haplotypes, providing definitive genetic proof that LRPPRC indeed causes LSFC.

nodes.description: Elevated blood and CSF lactate is a hallmark biochemical finding.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Decompensation (often with elevated lactate levels in blood and/or cerebrospinal fluid) is typically associated with developmental delay and/or regression.

meta.association: Maternally inherited MT-ATP6 nucleotide 8993 variants cause maternally inherited Leigh syndrome with heteroplasmy-dependent severity.

evidence.reference_title: NARP-MILS syndrome caused by 8993 T>G mitochondrial DNA mutation: a clinical, genetic and neuropathological study.
evidence.snippet: The 8993 T>G mutation in mitochondrial DNA has been associated with variable syndromes of differing severity ranging from maternally inherited Leigh's syndrome (MILS) to neuropathy, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa (NARP), depending on the mutation loads in affected patients.

nodes.description: Pathogenic MT-ATP6 variants, especially nucleotide 8993 substitutions, impair the proton-translocating sector of ATP synthase and produce maternally inherited Leigh syndrome when heteroplasmy is high.

evidence.reference_title: NARP-MILS syndrome caused by 8993 T>G mitochondrial DNA mutation: a clinical, genetic and neuropathological study.
evidence.snippet: The 8993 T>G mutation in mitochondrial DNA has been associated with variable syndromes of differing severity ranging from maternally inherited Leigh's syndrome (MILS) to neuropathy, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa (NARP), depending on the mutation loads in affected patients.

molecular_functions: proton-transporting ATP synthase activity, rotational mechanism

meta.association: Biallelic NDUFS4 loss-of-function variants cause complex I-deficient Leigh syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Structural insights into respiratory complex I deficiency and assembly from the mitochondrial disease-related ndufs4(-/-) mouse.
evidence.snippet: we use a popular complex I-linked mitochondrial disease model, the ndufs4-/- mouse, to define the structural, biochemical, and functional consequences of the absence of subunit NDUFS4.

meta.association: Biallelic variants in the nuclear-encoded complex I core subunit gene NDUFV1 cause complex I-deficient Leigh syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Leigh syndrome: One disorder, more than 75 monogenic causes.
evidence.snippet: This neurodegenerative disorder is genetically heterogeneous, and to date pathogenic mutations in >75 genes have been identified, encoded by 2 genomes (mitochondrial and nuclear).

nodes.description: The unifying biochemical lesion in Leigh syndrome is impaired mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, reducing ATP synthesis in tissues with high energy demand. Causative defects converge on the respiratory chain complexes (most often complex I), complex IV, ATP synthase (complex V), or upstream pyruvate oxidation.

biological_processes: proton motive force-driven mitochondrial ATP synthesis

evidence.reference_title: Leigh syndrome: One disorder, more than 75 monogenic causes.
evidence.snippet: Leigh syndrome is the most common pediatric presentation of mitochondrial disease.

evidence.reference_title: Leigh syndrome: One disorder, more than 75 monogenic causes.
evidence.snippet: This neurodegenerative disorder is genetically heterogeneous, and to date pathogenic mutations in >75 genes have been identified, encoded by 2 genomes (mitochondrial and nuclear).

meta.association: X-linked PDHA1 variants cause pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency presenting as Leigh syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical exome sequencing reveals a mutation in PDHA1 in Leigh syndrome: A case of a Chinese boy with lethal neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Leigh syndrome, the most common mitochondrial syndrome in pediatrics, has diverse clinical manifestations and is genetically heterogeneous.

nodes.description: X-linked PDHA1 and related pyruvate dehydrogenase complex defects block conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, depriving the TCA cycle of substrate and producing a Leigh phenotype with prominent lactic acidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical exome sequencing reveals a mutation in PDHA1 in Leigh syndrome: A case of a Chinese boy with lethal neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: A PDHA1 mutation (NM_000284.4:c.1167_1170del) was identified as the underlying cause.

meta.association: Biallelic SURF1 variants impair cytochrome c oxidase assembly and are a common cause of complex IV-deficient Leigh syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Mutations of SURF-1 in Leigh disease associated with cytochrome c oxidase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Sequence analysis of SURF-1 revealed mutations in numerous DNA samples from LD(COX-) patients, indicating that this gene is responsible for the major complementation group in this important mitochondrial disorder.

nodes.description: Seizures occur in a substantial fraction of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Neurologic features include hypotonia, spasticity, seizures, movement disorders, cerebellar ataxia, and peripheral neuropathy.

nodes.description: No disease-modifying therapy exists; management is supportive, including treatment of acidosis, seizures, dystonia, feeding support, and avoidance of mitochondrial toxins such as sodium valproate. GeneReviews advises that sodium valproate, medications that cause acidosis, and dichloroacetate should be avoided or used with caution, and that anesthesia requires careful consideration to avoid precipitating respiratory failure.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Treatment of manifestations: Treatment is supportive.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial DNA-Associated Leigh Syndrome Spectrum.
evidence.snippet: Sodium valproate, medications that cause acidosis, and dichloroacetate should be avoided or used with caution;
